description: EB-1A Extraordinary Ability intake playbook. Covers self-petitioning or employer-sponsored I-140, concurrent AOS, and consular processing.

I140_Only.label: I-140 Petition Only
I140_Only.notes: Premium processing available. Consular processing follows approval.

Concurrent_AOS.label: Concurrent I-140 & I-485 (Adjustment of Status)
Concurrent_AOS.notes: Priority date must be current for EB-1 to file concurrently.

required_facts.label: Beneficiary currently inside U.S. or abroad
Filing strategy (i140_only vs concurrent vs consular)
Intent to continue working in area of expertise
Beneficiary most recent entry: date, place, manner (I-94 / inspection)
Overstay, prior denials, removal/deportation, unlawful presence

identity.label: Statement detailing plans to continue work in area of expertise

escalation_flags.label: Fewer than 3 extraordinary ability criteria met
escalation_flags.message: Fewer than 3 statutory criteria identified. EB-1A requires at least 3, or a major one-time achievement.

escalation_flags.label: Weak evidence of sustained acclaim
escalation_flags.message: Limited independent evidence (letters, citations) of sustained national/international acclaim.

escalation_flags.label: Criminal history present
escalation_flags.message: Criminal history present. Requires attorney admissibility assessment.

escalation_flags.label: Adverse immigration history
escalation_flags.message: Adverse immigration history present. Assess inadmissibility bars.

escalation_flags.label: AOS may be barred by unlawful entry
escalation_flags.message: Last entry without inspection. AOS likely unavailable for EB-1A.

refinement_guidelines:
1. Case Overview
- What is your full legal name?
- Have you ever used another name, spelling, professional name, stage name, or pen name?
- What is your current occupation?
- How would you define your field of extraordinary ability?
- Is your proposed field narrower than your general industry?
- What is your principal area of specialization?
- How many years have you worked in this field?
- In which countries have you worked or received recognition?
- What do you consider your three to five most important professional achievements?
- Why do you believe you are among the small percentage at the top of your field?
- What distinguishes you from an ordinarily successful professional in the same occupation?
- Who would be the appropriate peer group against which your achievements should be compared?
- What objective data supports that comparison?
- Have you previously filed an O-1, EB-1A, EB-1B, EB-2 NIW, or another extraordinary-ability petition?
- Have you received any RFE, NOID, denial, revocation, or adverse immigration decision?

2. Filing Strategy and Immigration History
- Will the case be filed as a self-petition?
- Is an employer, agent, attorney, or another person filing on your behalf?
- Are you currently inside or outside the United States?
- What is your current immigration status?
- When does your current status expire?
- What was your most recent date of entry into the United States?
- Have you ever overstayed, worked without authorization, or violated your immigration status?
- Have you ever been arrested, charged, cited, or convicted?
- Have you ever made a misrepresentation in an immigration or visa application?
- Do you plan to file Form I-485 concurrently with Form I-140?
- Will you use consular processing instead?
- Do you intend to request premium processing?
- Do you have a previously approved employment-based petition with an earlier priority date?
- Is your spouse or child seeking derivative immigration benefits?
- Are there any urgent timing issues, such as status expiration, travel, employment changes, or aging-out concerns?

3. Proposed Work in the United States
- What work do you intend to perform in the United States?
- What position, projects, research, business, artistic work, athletic activity, or consulting will you pursue?
- Is this proposed work within the same field in which you earned acclaim?
- How is your proposed U.S. work connected to your previous achievements?
- Do you currently have a U.S. employer?
- Do you have an offer letter or employment agreement?
- Do you have consulting agreements, contracts, or statements of work?
- Do you have prospective clients or collaborators?
- Can those clients or collaborators provide letters of intent?
- Have you been invited to teach, speak, perform, research, advise, judge, or participate in projects in the United States?
- Are you launching or operating a U.S. business?
- Does the business have a business plan, funding, customers, revenue, employees, or strategic partners?
- What specific activities do you expect to complete during the first one to three years?
- What evidence demonstrates that these plans are realistic?
- Why is your continued work important to your field?
- Are there unexplained gaps between your past field and your proposed U.S. work?

4. One-Time Major International Achievement
- Have you received an award comparable in stature to a major internationally recognized prize?
- What is the exact name of the award?
- Who issued it?
- Was the award given to you individually or to a team or organization?
- What year did you receive it?
- How many candidates were considered?
- How many recipients were selected?
- Were candidates drawn from multiple countries?
- What were the selection criteria?
- Who served as judges or selectors?
- How are the judges recognized experts?
- What evidence establishes the award's international prestige?
- Has the award been covered by independent major media?
- Who were previous recipients?
- Can you prove that the award was for excellence in your field rather than participation, service, attendance, or popularity?
- For a team award, what evidence shows that you were personally recognized?

5. Criterion 1 - Prizes or Awards
- Please list every prize, award, honor, medal, or competitive distinction you have received.
- Which awards were received for excellence in your field?
- Which awards were national or international in scope?
- Were any awards limited to your employer, university, city, or local organization?
- Was the award competitive?
- Were you nominated, shortlisted, or selected as the winner?
- How many people were eligible?
- How many applied or were nominated?
- How many received the award?
- What were the written selection standards?
- Were the judges independent recognized experts?
- Is payment of a fee sufficient to obtain the award?
- Does ordinary membership, employment, graduation, or participation qualify a person for the award?
- Is there an official award website?
- Are there announcements, press releases, media reports, or archived pages?
- Can the awarding organization confirm your receipt in writing?
- If it was a team award, what was your personal contribution?
- Did the award produce additional recognition, invitations, promotions, or media coverage?
- Do you have the complete certificate and an English translation?
- Is the name or date on the award inconsistent with your résumé or other records?

6. Criterion 2 - Membership in Associations
- Please list all professional associations of which you are a member.
- What membership level do you hold?
- Is the membership open to anyone who pays dues?
- Is a degree, occupation, years of experience, or licensing status sufficient for admission?
- Does the association require outstanding achievements?
- Are the outstanding-achievement requirements written in the bylaws or membership rules?
- Who reviews membership applications?
- Are those reviewers recognized national or international experts?
- Were you nominated, invited, or independently selected?
- Did you submit evidence of achievements to qualify?
- What percentage of applicants is admitted?
- How many members hold your specific membership level?
- Is there a fellow, senior, elected, honorary, or selective tier that is stronger than ordinary membership?
- Can the association provide a letter explaining the admission process?
- Can you obtain the bylaws in effect when you were admitted?
- Does your membership certificate identify the membership class and admission date?
- Have the association's requirements changed since your admission?
- Can you prove that you remain a member in good standing?

7. Criterion 3 - Published Material About the Applicant
- Please identify every article, interview, profile, television segment, radio segment, podcast, or publication about you.
- Is the material primarily about you or your work?
- Does it merely mention your name?
- Was the article written independently?
- Was the content created by your employer, publicist, agent, or marketing department?
- Did you pay for or arrange the publication?
- Is it a press release, sponsored article, advertorial, or self-published post?
- What is the publication's name?
- What is the article's title?
- Who is the author?
- What is the publication date?
- Does the article clearly identify your work and achievements?
- Is the publication a professional publication, major trade publication, or other major media outlet?
- What are its circulation, readership, web traffic, distribution, or audience figures?
- Does the outlet have national or international reach?
- Can audience statistics be supported by independent sources?
- Do you have a complete copy rather than a screenshot of one paragraph?
- Is the webpage still available?
- Can the page be archived?
- Is an English translation required?
- Does the translation include the title, date, author, and publication name?
- Are there multiple articles that repeat the same original press release?
- What new recognition or opportunities resulted from the coverage?

8. Criterion 4 - Judging the Work of Others
- Have you reviewed journal manuscripts?
- Have you judged competitions, awards, exhibitions, performances, grants, business plans, or technical submissions?
- Have you served on a thesis, dissertation, hiring, promotion, or evaluation committee?
- Was the work you judged in your field or an allied field?
- Who invited you?
- Why were you selected as a judge?
- Was the invitation based on your expertise?
- Did you actually complete the judging?
- How many submissions did you evaluate?
- On what dates did you complete the reviews?
- Do you have confirmation from the organizer?
- Do you have reviewer-system screenshots or acknowledgment records?
- Do you have copies of evaluation forms or scoring sheets?
- Can confidential information be redacted while preserving proof of completed review?
- Was the judging part of your ordinary job duties?
- If it was part of your employment, why was it nevertheless selective or significant?
- What is the reputation of the journal, conference, institution, award, or competition?
- Was the judging national or international?
- Were the other judges recognized experts?
- Have you received repeated invitations?
- Can the organizer provide a verification letter?
- Is there evidence of completed judging beyond the initial invitation?

9. Criterion 5 - Original Contributions of Major Significance
- What are your most important original contributions?
- What exactly did you personally create, discover, design, develop, or introduce?
- How is each contribution original?
- When was it created?
- Was the work completed individually or by a team?
- What was your precise role?
- What objective evidence separates your contribution from that of your colleagues?
- Who has used, adopted, licensed, cited, commercialized, or implemented the contribution?
- Are those users independent of you and your employer?
- How many organizations or individuals have adopted it?
- In how many countries or markets has it been used?
- Did it generate revenue, savings, productivity gains, clinical outcomes, technical improvements, or market growth?
- Can those results be quantified?
- Are the figures supported by contracts, reports, analytics, financial statements, or independent records?
- Did the contribution influence industry standards or professional practices?
- Was it incorporated into products, protocols, guidelines, curricula, policies, or regulations?
- Has it been cited by other experts?
- Are the citations unusually high compared with similar work?
- Has the contribution received licensing interest?
- Does a patent exist?
- Has the patent been licensed, commercialized, cited, or implemented?
- What evidence demonstrates that the contribution is of major significance, not merely useful or original?
- Are there independent experts who can explain its significance?
- How do those experts know about the contribution?
- Have they used or relied on it?
- Can their statements be independently corroborated?
- Did the contribution lead to awards, grants, invitations, media coverage, or industry recognition?
- Can you provide before-and-after performance metrics?
- Are any impact claims based only on internal projections?
- Are there confidentiality restrictions preventing submission of the strongest evidence?

10. Criterion 6 - Authorship of Scholarly Articles
- Please provide a complete list of your published articles.
- Which publications are scholarly articles?
- Which are professional or major trade publications?
- Were the articles peer-reviewed?
- What subjects do they address?
- Are the subjects within your claimed EB-1A field?
- What was your contribution to each multi-author article?
- Are you the first author, corresponding author, senior author, or another co-author?
- Can a co-author or institution confirm your contribution?
- Where was each article published?
- Is the journal indexed?
- What is the journal's reputation?
- What was the publication date?
- What is the DOI or permanent URL?
- How many citations has each publication received?
- What citation database produced the figures?
- Are there duplicate citations across databases?
- How do your citation numbers compare with other researchers in the field?
- Are there highly cited papers, field-weighted metrics, or percentile rankings?
- Have the articles been relied upon by independent researchers, companies, or institutions?
- Have you authored book chapters, conference proceedings, technical standards, or professional articles?
- Are translations required?
- Do you possess complete copies of the publications?
- Are manuscripts listed as "submitted" or "under review" incorrectly presented as published work?

11. Criterion 7 - Artistic Exhibitions or Showcases
- Is your work artistic in nature?
- What work was displayed?
- Where was it displayed?
- Was the venue a gallery, museum, festival, exhibition, or recognized artistic showcase?
- On what dates was the work displayed?
- How were artists selected?
- Was participation juried, invited, competitive, or open to anyone?
- Who served as curator or selector?
- What is the reputation of the venue or event?
- How many artists participated?
- Was the exhibition local, national, or international?
- Was the exhibition covered by independent media or professional critics?
- Do catalogues or programs identify you and your work?
- Do you have photographs of the displayed work?
- Was the work sold?
- What audience or attendance data is available?
- Can a curator or gallery representative confirm your participation?
- Was the activity actually a conference presentation, product demonstration, or commercial trade show rather than an artistic exhibition?

12. Criterion 8 - Leading or Critical Role
- What was your exact title?
- What were your employment dates?
- Was your role leading, critical, or both?
- What decisions were you authorized to make?
- Did you supervise employees, budgets, departments, research, products, or strategic projects?
- Where were you positioned in the organizational hierarchy?
- Is there an organizational chart?
- To whom did you report?
- Who reported to you?
- What important function depended on your work?
- What would have happened if you had not performed that role?
- What measurable results did you produce?
- Did you generate revenue, reduce costs, obtain funding, create intellectual property, improve performance, or secure important clients?
- Can these results be corroborated by contemporaneous records?
- Were the results attributable specifically to you?
- Can a senior executive explain why your role was critical?
- Does the letter provide specific examples rather than repeating your job description?
- Did you receive promotions, retention incentives, special appointments, or internal recognition?
- Was your role important to the entire organization or only to a routine project?
- Does the organization have a distinguished reputation?
- What awards, rankings, funding, revenue, market position, major clients, or media coverage establish that reputation?
- Was the relevant department or division itself distinguished?
- Is the organization well known only locally?
- Are there independent sources supporting the organization's reputation?
- Does your title appear more senior in English than it was in the original language?

13. Criterion 9 - High Salary or Remuneration
- What was your base salary for each relevant year?
- What bonuses, equity, commissions, royalties, allowances, or other compensation did you receive?
- What was your total annual remuneration?
- Was the compensation actually paid or only offered?
- Do you have contracts, payroll records, tax records, bank deposits, or employer verification?
- In what currency were you paid?
- What exchange rate and date should be used?
- Was compensation affected by inflation or unusual currency conditions?
- Were benefits included in the stated amount?
- Is company revenue being incorrectly presented as your personal compensation?
- What is the appropriate comparison occupation?
- What is the appropriate geographic market?
- What is the appropriate level of seniority?
- What was the relevant comparison year?
- What reliable wage surveys or government data are available?
- Does the comparison use average, median, 75th percentile, 90th percentile, or another benchmark?
- Is your compensation significantly above the appropriate benchmark?
- Are comparator salaries for the same role and industry?
- If you are self-employed, how can personal remuneration be distinguished from business income?
- If compensation is confidential, can the records be redacted?
- Did your compensation decline or materially change?
- Are salary figures inconsistent across your tax returns, contracts, résumé, and employer letters?

14. Criterion 10 - Commercial Success in the Performing Arts
- What qualifying performing-arts work did you create or perform?
- What was your role in the production?
- What ticket, box-office, album, streaming, touring, licensing, or distribution results were achieved?
- What is the source of the commercial data?
- Can the figures be independently verified?
- How do the results compare with similar performers or productions?
- Were the results national or international?
- Did the work appear in recognized rankings or charts?
- What revenue was directly attributable to your participation?
- Were you a principal performer, creator, producer, director, or supporting participant?
- Is there evidence connecting the success specifically to you?
- Did the project receive independent media coverage?
- Are social-media views being presented without proof of commercial success?
- Are gross production revenues being confused with your individual compensation?
- Are the figures cumulative, estimated, or audited?

15. Comparable Evidence
- Which regulatory criterion does not readily apply to your occupation?
- Why does it not readily apply?
- What evidence do you propose as a substitute?
- What regulatory criterion is that evidence comparable to?
- How is it similar in quality and significance?
- Is comparable evidence necessary because the criterion is structurally inapplicable, or simply because you cannot satisfy it?
- Is the substitute evidence common as a measure of acclaim in your industry?
- Can recognized experts explain why the evidence demonstrates extraordinary ability?
- Are there objective benchmarks for the proposed comparable evidence?
- Can the evidence be evaluated without creating an entirely new criterion?
- Have standard criteria that do apply to the occupation been fully analyzed first?

16. Sustained National or International Acclaim
- When did you first receive significant recognition?
- What is the most recent evidence of recognition?
- Has your acclaim continued over multiple years?
- Are there unexplained periods with little or no activity?
- Have you continued to publish, judge, lead, perform, innovate, or receive recognition?
- Have you received repeated invitations from independent organizations?
- Has the geographic scope of your recognition expanded?
- Is your recognition limited to one employer or institution?
- Do independent professionals know and use your work?
- Has your acclaim continued after changing employers?
- Are your strongest achievements too old to show current acclaim?
- What current evidence demonstrates that your reputation remains active?
- Has the recognition occurred nationally, internationally, or only locally?
- What evidence shows that recognition is attributable to your personal achievements?

17. Small Percentage at the Top of the Field
- How large is your field?
- How should the field be defined without making it artificially narrow?
- Who are your genuine professional peers?
- What metrics are normally used to identify top professionals in this field?
- Where do you rank under those metrics?
- What percentile evidence is available?
- Are there citation, salary, revenue, adoption, award-selectivity, audience, or ranking comparisons?
- How many professionals have achieved similar results?
- Are your accomplishments unusual at your career stage?
- Have you achieved recognition beyond your employer's internal environment?
- Are you sought out by other experts?
- Do others invite you to judge, advise, speak, collaborate, or lead?
- Has your work changed or influenced your field?
- Would independent experts describe you as being at the top of the field?
- What objective evidence supports those descriptions?
- Is the case based primarily on the number of documents rather than the quality of achievements?

18. Recommendation and Expert Letters
- What is the recommender's full name and title?
- What are the recommender's professional qualifications?
- Is the recommender independent of you?
- Has the recommender employed, supervised, studied with, or collaborated with you?
- How did the recommender learn about your work?
- Has the recommender personally used, cited, reviewed, adopted, or evaluated your work?
- What specific contribution can the recommender discuss?
- What objective facts can the recommender verify?
- Can those facts be corroborated by exhibits?
- Can the recommender compare you with others in the field?
- Can the recommender explain why your contribution is of major significance?
- Can the recommender explain the reputation of an organization, award, publication, or project?
- Will the letter disclose the nature of the relationship?
- Does the recommender have a résumé or professional biography?
- Can the letter be placed on official letterhead?
- Can the recommender sign and date it?
- Does the letter avoid unsupported adjectives and conclusory praise?
- Do multiple letters repeat identical language?
- Are too many letters from the same employer, institution, or collaborator?
- Can at least some letters come from experts who have never directly worked with you?

19. Document Authenticity and Source Reliability
- Who created the document?
- When was it created?
- Was it created contemporaneously with the underlying event?
- Is it an original record, certified copy, screenshot, recreated document, or self-prepared summary?
- Can it be verified through an official website or independent source?
- Does it contain complete names, dates, titles, and issuing information?
- Is any portion cropped, edited, translated, or redacted?
- Is the original version available?
- Is a certified English translation required?
- Can the issuing organization confirm authenticity?
- Does the document conflict with another exhibit?
- Is a website printout missing its URL and access date?
- Is the evidence based on a temporary webpage that should be archived?
- Does the document contain confidential information requiring redaction?
- Is it permissible to disclose the evidence?
- Does the document prove the claimed fact directly, or only imply it?

20. Missing-Evidence Follow-Up
- What fact are we trying to prove?
- Which EB-1A criterion does the fact support?
- What document currently supports it?
- Is the evidence objective or testimonial?
- Is the evidence independent?
- What additional document would provide stronger corroboration?
- Who controls that document?
- Can it be requested from an employer, association, publisher, organizer, client, or government body?
- Is there a deadline for obtaining it?
- Is an alternative source available?
- Can a declaration explain why primary evidence is unavailable?
- Does the missing record materially weaken the criterion?
- Should the criterion be retained, supplemented, or omitted?

21. Résumé and Timeline Consistency
- Are all employment dates consistent across the résumé, letters, contracts, and immigration forms?
- Are job titles translated consistently?
- Are degree dates and institution names consistent?
- Are publication dates and citation counts accurate?
- Are awards listed under the correct year?
- Are there overlapping full-time jobs or unexplained employment gaps?
- Are project results claimed before the project began?
- Are company names consistent after mergers, acquisitions, or name changes?
- Are compensation figures consistent with tax and payroll records?
- Are media articles described accurately?
- Are memberships current, expired, or honorary?
- Are pending patents or unpublished papers clearly identified as pending?
- Are estimated metrics clearly distinguished from verified metrics?
- Does any document exaggerate the applicant's title, role, authorship, or impact?

22. Prior Filing and Public-Record Risks
- Have you previously described your field differently in another immigration filing?
- Have you previously submitted a résumé with different dates or titles?
- Have you claimed different authorship, job duties, salary, or achievements elsewhere?
- Are your LinkedIn profile, employer website, personal website, and published biography consistent?
- Are there public records contradicting the petition?
- Have any articles, awards, memberships, or organizations been criticized as paid, predatory, or nonselective?
- Have any publications been retracted?
- Have any patents expired, been abandoned, or never been implemented?
- Have citation counts been artificially inflated or incorrectly calculated?
- Are any recommendation letters written by people who cannot verify their statements?
- Are there undisclosed family, financial, ownership, or employment relationships with recommenders?
- Have prior petitions received findings concerning credibility or misrepresentation?
- Is any evidence AI-generated, reconstructed, edited, or unverifiable?
- Is any evidence being submitted without the applicant's ability to explain it accurately?
